note: Deduped MCQ item bank for Israeli ROC Part A. Each item lists every source exam it appears in (sourceExams). Items whose explanation ends with (*) carry a 'note' flagging where the exam's expected answer drifts from international real-world practice; the exam key is preserved per project policy. Option keys map a=א b=ב c=ג d=ד e=ה f=ו.

items.prompt: Which feature is NOT found in a 406 MHz satellite EPIRB?

options.text: It transmits a homing signal on 121.5 MHz
A voice homing signal
Emergency transmission on 406.025 MHz
Automatic release and float-free mechanism

items.explanation: A 406 MHz EPIRB has no voice capability. Its homing aid is a low-power 121.5 MHz RF signal for direction-finding by SAR units, not a spoken/voice signal. The 406.025 MHz burst carries the digital distress data, and float-free release is standard.

items.prompt: The radiotelephone urgency signal is:

options.text: MAYDAY
PAN PAN
SECURITE
SEELONCE FEENEE

items.explanation: PAN PAN is the urgency signal (safety of a vessel/person, not yet grave and imminent). MAYDAY is distress, SECURITE is safety, and SEELONCE FEENEE ends distress traffic.

items.prompt: Which publication should a GMDSS operator consult regarding communication procedures in distress?

options.text: IMO recommendations
Manufacturer instruction manuals
Ministry of Transport procedures
ITU procedures

items.explanation: Distress communication procedures are defined by the ITU Radio Regulations (and the ITU Manual for Use by the Maritime Mobile and Maritime Mobile-Satellite Services).

items.prompt: Distress messages are:

options.text: Meteorological warnings
Navigation warnings
Man overboard
All answers are correct

items.explanation: Distress applies only to grave and imminent danger requiring immediate assistance — e.g. man overboard. Meteorological and navigation warnings are safety (SECURITE) traffic, not distress.

items.prompt: How does the station controlling a distress announce that the distress situation has ended?

options.text: It uses the term MAYDAY PRUDONCE
It announces "No Further Assistance Required"
It uses the term SEELONCE FEENEE
It sends a distress cancellation on DSC Channel 70

items.explanation: SEELONCE FEENEE ("silence finished") ends distress traffic and lifts radio silence. PRUDONCE relaxes silence to careful working while distress continues; it does not end it.

items.prompt: Which VHF working channel would you propose to Haifa coast station for connecting a call to a shore subscriber?

options.text: CH06
CH70
CH24
CH22

items.explanation: Ch24 is a public-correspondence (duplex) working channel used for shore-link telephone calls via the coast station. Ch06 is intership simplex, Ch70 is DSC, and Ch22 is not a standard public-correspondence channel here.

items.prompt: What determines VHF range?

options.text: The transmission channel
Receiver sensitivity
Answers b + d are correct
Height of the ship's transmitting antenna

items.explanation: VHF propagates by line of sight, so antenna height (d) is the dominant range factor; receiver sensitivity (b) is a contributing factor, which is why the exam combines them. (*) Real-world teaching treats antenna height as the primary determinant — the combined answer is exam-specific.
items.note: (*) Antenna height is the dominant line-of-sight factor; folding in receiver sensitivity is exam-specific. Key 'c' preserved per policy.

items.prompt: An EPIRB has fallen overboard and is lost. Which step do you take?

options.text: Transmit a false-alert cancellation to the nearest MCC and log it
Transmit a false-alert cancellation to the nearest coast station and log it
Transmit an urgency DSC message to cancel the distress via a coast station
Transmit a false-alert cancellation on Channel 16 to all nearby stations

items.explanation: A lost EPIRB may immerse and self-activate, sending a false distress. Notify the nearest coast station so the alert can be cancelled with the authorities, and record it in the log.

items.prompt: A ship's call sign is:

options.text: NURIT
PYCH
VCH4
Answers b + c are correct

items.explanation: A call sign is an alphanumeric identifier issued by the administration, unlike a vessel name (NURIT). (*) Both PYCH and VCH4 read as valid call-sign formats, so option d is arguably more defensible; the exam key 'b' is preserved per policy.
items.note: (*) Ambiguous: both 'b' and 'c' look like well-formed call signs, so 'd' (b+c) is arguably more correct. Key 'b' preserved per policy.

items.prompt: How does a NAVTEX station transmit over one day?

options.text: Every 6 hours, at most 15 minutes per transmission
Every 4 hours, at most 10 minutes per transmission
4 times, each as long as needed to complete the messages
6 times, at most 1 minute per transmission

items.explanation: Under the international time-sharing plan each NAVTEX station is allocated a 10-minute slot every 4 hours (six slots per day) to avoid co-channel interference on 518 kHz.

items.prompt: What does AIS stand for?

options.text: Auto Insert Signal
Automatic Information System
Automatic Identity Signal
Automatic Identification System

items.explanation: AIS = Automatic Identification System, which exchanges identity, position, course and speed between vessels and shore for collision avoidance and traffic monitoring. (A separate bank item carries the variant where 'Automatic Identification System' is option b.)

items.prompt: What action do you take after transmitting a false alert on VHF (DSC)?

options.text: Send a DSC message on Channel 70 to cancel the false alert
Make a voice cancellation message and transmit it on Channel 16
Transmit a voice cancellation on Channel 13
Transmit a voice cancellation on Channel 24

items.explanation: Cancel a VHF DSC false distress alert by voice on Ch16: "ALL STATIONS (x3), THIS IS [name/callsign/MMSI], CANCEL MY DISTRESS ALERT of [time]."

items.prompt: Urgency priority is used for:

options.text: Messages concerning safety of life at sea (SOLAS)
Messages handling important navigation messages
Messages containing information concerning the safety of a mobile unit or a person
Messages concerning on-scene communication

items.explanation: Urgency (PAN PAN) concerns the safety of a vessel or person where danger is not yet grave and imminent (which would be distress / MAYDAY).

items.prompt: When is transmitting a safety signal justified?

options.text: When there is a threat to the ship's security
When there is danger to the safety of a person or property on the ship
When there are meteorological warnings or navigation warnings
When the NAVTEX system is not working

items.explanation: SECURITE (safety) precedes navigational and meteorological warnings — e.g. a hazard to navigation or a gale warning.

items.prompt: Under GMDSS, mandatory VHF-DSC and radiotelephone equipment must be tested at a minimum interval of:

options.text: Daily
Annually, by a Ministry of Transport representative
At the annual SOLAS survey
Monthly

items.explanation: A daily functional check of the VHF-DSC and radiotelephone is required and recorded in the radio log. (Contrast: DSC internal self-test weekly; EPIRB monthly.)

items.prompt: Which equipment is used to send distress alerts and calls to designated stations?

options.text: NAVTEX
GPS
DSC controller
Scanning watch-keeping receiver

items.explanation: The DSC (Digital Selective Calling) controller initiates digital distress alerts and calls addressed to specific (designated) stations. NAVTEX is receive-only MSI; a watch receiver only listens.

items.prompt: Where is the radio log kept?

options.text: In the captain's office
In the operator's room
At the position from which the station is operated
Anywhere aboard the vessel

items.explanation: The radio log is kept at the operating position of the radio station so entries can be made as communications occur.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of the VHF Bridge-to-Bridge service?

options.text: Communication between ships to enhance their navigational safety
Communication between ships and coast stations to enhance navigational safety
Communication between yachts and marinas to enhance navigational safety
Communication between ships during search and rescue (SAR)

items.explanation: Bridge-to-bridge is ship-to-ship navigation-safety communication (collision avoidance, passing arrangements), typically on Ch13.

items.prompt: On which channel(s) will you be contacted during search and rescue?

options.text: CH16, CH75
CH08, CH70
CH17, CH15
CH06, CH16

items.explanation: On-scene VHF coordination uses Ch16 (calling/distress) and Ch06 (intership / on-scene). Ch70 is DSC, Ch15/17 are low-power on-board, Ch75 is a guard band.

items.prompt: Under what circumstances should a TR (transit report) be transmitted?

options.text: When there is danger to the safety of navigation
When entering and leaving port
During a danger situation
Answers a + b are correct

items.explanation: A TR (transit/traffic report) is passed to the coast station on entering and leaving port to report passage details, aiding traffic management and SAR readiness.

items.prompt: What does describing VHF transmission as "line-of-sight range" mean?

options.text: VHF is effective only with nearby stations that are within visual range
The ship's antenna height can affect the communication radius
The typical range is 25 miles
Answers b + c are correct

items.explanation: VHF propagates roughly line-of-sight, so range grows with antenna height and is in the order of ~25 NM in practice — both b and c describe this.

items.prompt: When a GMDSS operator on watch hears SECURITE spoken three times, the information to follow is:

options.text: A message concerning the safety of navigation or a meteorological warning
Safety of a vessel or person in danger
A vessel needs immediate assistance
A coast station traffic list

items.explanation: SECURITE (x3) is the safety signal, preceding navigational or meteorological warnings. Danger to a vessel/person would be PAN PAN (urgency) or MAYDAY (distress).

items.prompt: Which was allocated as the distress continuous-watch frequency that must be on all survival craft?

options.text: Channel 6
Channel 13
Channel 16
Channel 70

items.explanation: Ch16 (156.8 MHz) is the VHF distress, urgency, safety and calling channel kept under continuous watch.

items.prompt: The number 428000500 is:

options.text: A ship-station DSC MMSI
An MMSI belonging to a country with MID 428
An MMSI of a group of DSC stations
Answers a + b are correct

items.explanation: A 9-digit MMSI with the MID in the first three digits (428 = Israel) and no leading zeros identifies an individual ship station — so it is both a ship MMSI (a) and belongs to MID 428 (b).

items.prompt: A VHF transmitter can reduce its output power down to:

options.text: 0.1 Watt
1 Watt
5 Watt
10 Watt

items.explanation: Fixed VHF sets switch between high power (25 W) and low power (1 W); low power is used for short-range/port working to reduce interference.

items.prompt: A distress alert transmitted via DSC must include:

options.text: MMSI, nature of distress, position and time of the last update
Nature of distress, ship's name, working channel with the coast station
Assistance required, call sign, nature of distress
MMSI, assistance required, last position (manual or from GPS) and time

items.explanation: A DSC distress alert carries the vessel's identity (MMSI), the nature of distress, and the position with the time it was valid. (A separate exam variant phrases the position field as 'manual or from GPS' and keys answer d.)

items.prompt: Who determines the position of an EPIRB operating in the COSPAS-SARSAT system?

options.text: The MCC, from the transmission it receives from the LUT
The LUT, from the transmission it receives from the satellite
The satellite computes the position itself
Only the EPIRB, provided it has an integrated GPS

items.explanation: The LUT (Local User Terminal) processes the satellite downlink (Doppler and/or encoded GPS data) to compute the beacon position, then passes it to the MCC and on to the RCC.

items.prompt: What does this message on Channel 16 mean? "All Ships (x3) This is Haifa Radio (x3) Traffic List on Channel 25"

options.text: Haifa coast station has important safety traffic
Haifa coast station has traffic for several vessels and is announcing it via a traffic list, to be transmitted on Channel 25
Haifa coast station warns all vessels of dense traffic; listen on Channel 25 for warnings
None of the answers is correct

items.explanation: A traffic list is a coast station's call announcing it holds messages for listed vessels; the actual traffic is worked on the stated channel (here Ch25).

items.prompt: Which of the following is a GMDSS functional requirement?

options.text: Bridge-to-Bridge communication
Receiving weather-fax chart broadcasts
Both of the above
None of the above

items.explanation: Bridge-to-bridge (ship-to-ship navigation-safety) communication is one of the nine GMDSS functional requirements. Weather-fax reception is not a GMDSS functional requirement.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of the AIS system?

options.text: To serve as a replacement for the DSC system
To provide vessels safety information as a replacement for NAVTEX
To reduce traffic congestion, enhance navigational safety and prevent collisions between vessels
To increase the efficiency of the ship's radar

items.explanation: AIS broadcasts identity, position, course and speed so vessels and shore can build a traffic picture for collision avoidance and traffic management.

items.prompt: What action do you take after transmitting a false alert on DSC?

options.text: Send a DSC message on Channel 70 to cancel the false alert
Turn off the device, prepare a voice cancellation message and transmit it on Channel 16
Transmit a voice cancellation on Channel 13
Transmit a voice cancellation on Channel 24

items.explanation: Stop the alert, then cancel it by voice on Ch16 to all stations, identifying your vessel and the time of the false alert.

items.prompt: Which publications are required aboard under GMDSS requirements for an international voyage?

options.text: The List of Coast Stations
The alphabetical List of Ship Stations
The List of Call Signs and Numerical Identities
All of the above

items.explanation: Carriage of the ITU lists (Coast Stations, Ship Stations, and Call Signs & Numerical Identities) supports correct addressing, identification and charges.

items.prompt: Urgency priority is used for:

options.text: A request for casualty (medical) evacuation
Messages handling important navigation messages
Messages containing information concerning the safety of a mobile unit or a person
Messages concerning on-scene communication

items.explanation: A medical-evacuation request is urgency (PAN PAN) traffic concerning the safety of a person. (*) Option c is also a textbook definition of urgency; this paper keys the casualty-evacuation example.
items.note: (*) Both 'a' (casualty evacuation) and 'c' (safety of a mobile unit/person) are legitimate urgency uses; the exam keys 'a'. Preserved per policy.

items.prompt: The radiotelephone safety signal is:

options.text: SECURITE repeated three times
SAFETY SAFETY SAFETY
PAN PAN repeated three times
SECURITE SECURITE repeated three times

items.explanation: The safety signal is the word SECURITE spoken three times before a safety announcement.

items.prompt: The code MA33 can indicate:

options.text: The call sign of a radiotelephony ship
A NAVTEX message identity (navigation warning no. 33 from station M)
A coast station whose country identity letters are MA
A DSC alert code relating to a navigation warning

items.explanation: A NAVTEX technical code is B1B2B3B4: B1 = transmitter station (M), B2 = subject indicator (A = navigational warning), B3B4 = serial number (33).

items.prompt: Regarding channels 15 and 17:

options.text: Transmission is permitted only at 1 Watt on these channels
These channels are for ship safety only
They are duplex channels for vessel traffic control
Answers a + b are correct

items.explanation: Ch15 and Ch17 are limited to 1 W and are used for on-board (intra-ship) communications.

items.prompt: Which VHF channels do you use for on-board (internal) communications on a vessel?

options.text: CH79 and CH11
CH15 and CH17
CH13 and CH15
CH11 and CH13

items.explanation: Ch15 and Ch17 are the low-power (1 W) on-board communication channels.

items.prompt: The expression PAN PAN means:

options.text: A safety message requesting second priority in communications
An urgency message requesting second priority in communications
A distress message requesting first priority in communications
An urgency message — man overboard

items.explanation: PAN PAN is the urgency signal — second only to distress (MAYDAY) in priority.

items.prompt: What does the DSC controller do when the operator does not key in updated information while operating in distress-alert mode?

options.text: It begins transmitting and sounds a beep that can only be silenced manually
It begins the distress alert, but rescuers will be unable to identify the ship's name, position and status
The distress alert begins and default data is transmitted
It calls the vessel in distress to obtain its identity, position and status

items.explanation: The alert still goes out using the controller's stored/default values (MMSI, last GPS position if interfaced, undesignated nature) — which is why keeping position current matters.

items.prompt: What is the role of the audible alarm in a SART?

options.text: It tells survivors that help is probably nearby
It tells survivors the battery charge is getting low
It tells survivors it is switching to standby
It tells survivors a nearby vessel is signalling on DSC

items.explanation: When a SART is interrogated by a radar, its audible/visual indicator tells survivors a radar (i.e. a potential rescuer) is within range.

items.prompt: How can the range of a SART be increased as much as possible?

options.text: Put the SART in the water immediately on activation
Mount the SART as high as possible
Operate the SART at maximum power
Calibrate the SART's transmit frequency using its indicator light

items.explanation: SART range is line-of-sight to the interrogating radar, so height is decisive — mount it as high as practical above the water.

items.prompt: On which of the following channels is transmission permitted only at a maximum power of 1 Watt?

options.text: CH14, CH12
CH70, CH06
CH15, CH17
CH17, CH67

items.explanation: Ch15 and Ch17 are restricted to 1 W (on-board comms).

items.prompt: Which statement is NOT correct regarding the handheld survival-craft VHF?

options.text: It operates FM/VHF on Channel 16
Its maximum radiated transmit power is 2 Watt
It operates in SIMPLEX only
All the answers are correct

items.explanation: Survival-craft portable VHF works simplex on Ch16 plus at least one other channel; its effective radiated power is low (about 0.25 W minimum) — the '2 W maximum' statement is the incorrect one.

items.prompt: What does AIS stand for?

options.text: Auto Insert Signal
Automatic Identification System
Automatic Information System
No correct answer

items.explanation: AIS = Automatic Identification System. (This is the option arrangement where the correct text is option b; another bank item carries the arrangement where it is option d.)

items.prompt: What should you do when a COSPAS-SARSAT EPIRB has been activated by mistake?

options.text: Notify all nearby stations of the false alert on Channel 16
Notify the nearest LUT of the false alert on Channel 16
Notify the nearest MCC of the false alert on Channel 16
Notify a nearby coast station or RCC of the false alert on Channel 16

items.explanation: Cancel an accidental beacon activation by informing a coast station or RCC (the SAR authority), who can call off the response.

items.prompt: Which was allocated in GMDSS for VHF on-scene communication?

options.text: Channel 24
Channels 21 and 82
Channel 70
Channel 16

items.explanation: Ch16 (with Ch06) is used for VHF on-scene SAR communication; Ch70 is DSC and the others are working/public-correspondence channels.

items.prompt: Which expression indicates the end of a distress situation?

options.text: SEELONCE MAYDAY
SEELONCE FEENEE
SEELONCE DISTRESS
SEELONCE OUT

items.explanation: SEELONCE FEENEE announces that distress traffic has ended and normal working may resume.

items.prompt: Into how many safety levels are NAVTEX messages divided?

options.text: Vital, Important, Routine
DISTRESS, URGENCY, SAFETY
All messages have equal priority unless they concern distress
None of the answers is correct

items.explanation: NAVTEX uses three handling priorities: Vital (printed immediately), Important, and Routine.

items.prompt: In which of the following events is safety priority normally used?

options.text: A crew member overboard
A serious medical situation involving a crew member
Answers a + b
Events concerning the safety of navigation or a meteorological warning

items.explanation: Safety (SECURITE) covers navigational and meteorological warnings. Man overboard is distress; a serious medical case is urgency.

items.prompt: Which statement is correct regarding the GMDSS equipment requirement for a vessel sailing only in Area A1?

options.text: The vessel must carry radio equipment capable of initiating a ship-to-shore distress alert from the navigating bridge
VHF-DSC can be the sole means for transmitting a distress alert
MF or HF DSC can satisfy the equipment requirement
HF SSB incorporating MSI messages can satisfy the equipment requirement

items.explanation: The functional requirement is to be able to initiate a ship-to-shore distress alert from the bridge. (*) Option b is also true for Area A1 (VHF-DSC may indeed be the sole alerting means); this paper keys the more general functional statement 'a'.
items.note: (*) Both 'a' and 'b' are true for an A1-only vessel; the exam keys the general functional-requirement statement 'a'. Preserved per policy.

items.prompt: What is Channel 16 used for?

options.text: Messages relating to NAVTEX broadcasts
Safety warnings by automatic calling
Distress, urgency, safety and calling communications
Weather messages

items.explanation: Ch16 is the VHF distress, urgency, safety and calling channel (voice).

items.prompt: Urgency communications include the following items:

options.text: Communications about assisting/towing a vessel restricted in its ability to manoeuvre
Communications about the medical condition of a crew member or passenger needing medical assistance
Any medical-evacuation request
All the answers are correct

items.explanation: Urgency (PAN PAN) covers safety of a vessel/person not in grave and imminent danger — including towing of a disabled vessel and medical assistance/evacuation.

items.prompt: What type of communication is the On-Scene Communications service intended for?

options.text: Internal communications during a voyage
Communications between a mobile unit (vessel or aircraft) in distress and the mobile stations rendering assistance
Ship-to-ship communications about navigation safety
Communications permitted in ports only

items.explanation: On-scene communications take place at the distress location, between the unit in distress and the units assisting it (typically Ch16/Ch06).

items.prompt: A ship's survival-craft call sign would be:

options.text: SKTY26
SKTY2
S2KTY6
SKT626

items.explanation: A survival craft's identity is formed from the parent ship's call sign (SKTY) followed by two digits — SKTY26.

items.prompt: Immediately on leaving a marina or port, I will carry out the following communication:

options.text: Contact all nearby coast stations to ask whether they have any traffic for me
Broadcast to all nearby vessels that I am ready to receive any traffic from them
Send a TR to the coast station nearest my departure point
Send a DSC call to some coast station to test the equipment

items.explanation: On departure you send a transit report (TR) to the relevant coast station to report your passage.

items.prompt: How do you respond to a safety message concerning a firing/gunnery area?

options.text: Send an acknowledgement of receipt to the station that transmitted the safety message
Record the message details in the station log and immediately pass the message to the watch-keeper
Transmit a PAN PAN message to warn nearby vessels
No answer is correct

items.explanation: Safety (SECURITE) broadcasts are not acknowledged; you log them and inform the officer of the watch so it can be acted on.

items.prompt: On which channels would a SAR vessel communicate with a station in distress?

options.text: CH08, CH26
CH76, CH14
CH16, CH06
CH24, CH75

items.explanation: On-scene SAR voice uses Ch16 (calling/distress) and Ch06 (intership/on-scene).

items.prompt: Which communication channels are used between fishing vessels and a station in Tel Aviv-Yafo?

options.text: CH79, CH11
CH25, CH24
CH13, CH76
CH73, CH67

items.explanation: Local assignment: fishing-vessel working with the Tel Aviv station uses Ch73 and Ch67.

items.prompt: Which features may be found in a COSPAS-SARSAT-type satellite beacon?

options.text: Transmission on 121.5 MHz for homing purposes
Automatic release and float-free mechanism
Emergency transmission on 406.025 MHz
All the answers are correct

items.explanation: A 406 MHz EPIRB transmits the digital alert on 406.025 MHz, carries a 121.5 MHz homing signal, and is typically float-free with a hydrostatic release.

items.prompt: DSC controllers have a function allowing an internal test without transmitting. How often should such a test be carried out on the equipment?

options.text: Once a day
Once a week
Twice a week
Once a month

items.explanation: Per IMO guidance the DSC equipment is tested daily by internal means without radiating; a test call to another station is done weekly.

items.prompt: Which statement is NOT correct regarding the SART?

options.text: It operates in the S-Band, and its transmit power can be switched between Low and High
Its battery should allow operation for 96 hours from activation
A typical range, if mounted about 1 m above the water, is 4-6 nautical miles
Both vessels and aircraft can home on it

items.explanation: A radar-SART works in the X-Band (9 GHz) with fixed output — it is not an S-Band, power-switchable device, so statement a is the incorrect one.

items.prompt: The radar transponder (SART) transmits when:

options.text: Immediately when it is manually activated
The SART never transmits, it only receives radar signals
An X-Band radar within its reception range is operating
The SART detects S-Band radar transmissions

items.explanation: Once armed, the SART stays in standby and only responds (transmits) when interrogated by a nearby X-Band (9 GHz) radar.

items.prompt: You received an urgency alert on Channel 70 addressed to all stations. What do you do?

options.text: Acknowledge it immediately on DSC
It is not possible to receive urgency alerts on DSC
Switch to Channel 16 and listen to the urgency message
Wait on Channel 70 for the acknowledgement of the RCC operating in the area

items.explanation: An all-stations urgency DSC announcement is not acknowledged by DSC; you switch to the working/voice channel (Ch16) to hear the message.

items.prompt: Which of the following combinations indicates an MMSI of a group of non-Israeli vessels?
items.explanation: A group-ship MMSI has a single leading zero (0MIDxxxxx). 048211111 is a group call for MID 482 (not Israel's 428).

items.prompt: The inter-ship channel designated for navigation-safety purposes:

options.text: CH11
CH06
CH17
CH13

items.explanation: Ch13 is the bridge-to-bridge navigation-safety channel between vessels.

items.prompt: The identity 023111428 refers to:

options.text: A coast station with MID 213
A group of Israeli vessels
A group of vessels whose MID is 231
A vessel belonging to a country with MID 023

items.explanation: A single leading zero marks a group-ship call; the following three digits are the MID (231), so it is a vessel group with MID 231.

items.prompt: A radiotelephone-equipped ship keeps a listening watch on Channel 16 on the high seas:

options.text: Whenever there is a crew member next to the VHF set
No watch is needed because DSC removes the requirement
24 hours a day
8 hours a day

items.explanation: Ch16 listening watch is maintained continuously (24 hours) while at sea.

items.prompt: Which statement about the NAVTEX installation is correct?

options.text: NAVTEX messages can relate to warnings of hurricanes
The NAVTEX installation can both receive and transmit Vital/Important/Routine messages
You may request a repeat of a faulty message from a coast station when the error rate exceeds 4.9%
The NAVTEX range is 20-40 NM and it covers only Area A1

items.explanation: NAVTEX is receive-only MSI and can carry tropical-storm/hurricane warnings. It does not transmit, its range is ~200-400 NM, and rejected/garbled messages are simply re-received on the next scheduled transmission.

items.prompt: Which of the following is an on-board (internal) communication channel?

options.text: CH12
CH13
CH14
CH15

items.explanation: Ch15 (with Ch17) is a low-power on-board communication channel; Ch12/13/14 are port-operations/bridge-to-bridge.

items.prompt: How do you avoid receiving irrelevant and unnecessary messages on the NAVTEX receiver?

options.text: Operate it only when near the desired station
Operate it according to the exact schedule of the station you want information from
Arrange in advance with the desired coast station before the voyage
Program it to suppress unwanted stations/areas and unwanted message types

items.explanation: You program the receiver to select the stations/areas and the message subject categories you want, suppressing the rest (except non-rejectable categories A, B, D, L).

items.prompt: The term MAYDAY RELAY refers to:

options.text: A vessel telling a station in distress that it is ready to assist it
Passing a safety message from a coast station to all nearby vessels
Announcing the end of a distress via a coast station
Transmission of a distress message by a station that is not itself in distress

items.explanation: A MAYDAY RELAY is a distress alert/message transmitted on behalf of another unit by a station that is not itself in distress.

items.prompt: The following message is heard on CH16: "Haifa Radio This is Clement TR Over". It means:

options.text: Haifa coast station has important safety traffic and is passing it to the vessel Clement
Haifa coast station has traffic for the coast station in Cyprus
The vessel Clement is telling Haifa coast station on CH16 that it intends to send it passage (transit) details
Haifa is transmitting a traffic list and the vessel Clement is on the list

items.explanation: The vessel Clement is calling Haifa Radio to pass a TR (transit report) — its passage details.

items.prompt: The following channels are used for on-board (internal) communications:

options.text: CH12, CH20
CH12, CH14
CH67, CH73
CH17, CH15

items.explanation: Ch15 and Ch17 are the 1 W on-board communication channels.

items.prompt: Which is the correct statement?

options.text: About every 90 minutes an Inmarsat communication satellite passes over my vessel
A COSPAS-SARSAT satellite measures the Doppler shift and transmits this value to the LUT
The Doppler shift determines the EPIRB's identity
Answers b and c are correct

items.explanation: The low-earth-orbit COSPAS-SARSAT satellite relays the beacon signal (with its Doppler shift) to the LUT, which computes the position. Doppler gives position, not identity; Inmarsat satellites are geostationary, not passing every 90 minutes.

items.prompt: On channel 26 Haifa Radio tells you: "Loreta this is Haifa Radio Your Turn is 4 Stand-by". You should:

options.text: Keep a dual watch on CH26 and CH16 until the coast station calls you back
Keep a continuous watch on CH16 until the coast station calls you back
Drop the link and make a new call in 4 minutes
Listen for 4 minutes on CH26 then disconnect and re-establish on the calling channel

items.explanation: You are number 4 in the queue: stand by, keeping watch on the working channel (Ch26) and Ch16, until the coast station calls you for your turn.

items.prompt: An EPIRB has fallen overboard and is lost. You take the following steps:

options.text: Transmit a false-alert cancellation to the nearest RCC and log it
Transmit a false-alert cancellation to the nearest coast station and log it
Transmit an urgency DSC message to cancel the distress via a coast station
Transmit a false-alert cancellation on Channel 16 to all nearby stations

items.explanation: A lost EPIRB may self-activate in the water; notify the nearest coast station so the alert can be cancelled, and record it in the log.

items.prompt: The possible identifications of a vessel in communications are:

options.text: Ship's name, owning company's name, port of registry
Ship's name, MMSI, port of registry
International call sign, MMSI, ship's name
EPIRB serial number, MMSI, ship's name

items.explanation: A vessel is identified in radio by its name, its international call sign, and its MMSI.

items.prompt: An Inmarsat-E type satellite beacon operates in the band:

options.text: S-Band
X-Band
L-Band
C-Band

items.explanation: Inmarsat-E EPIRBs operated in the L-Band (~1.6 GHz) via geostationary Inmarsat satellites.

items.prompt: The means of communication between a towing ship and a towed yacht:

options.text: CH70
CH15, CH17
CH06
CH13

items.explanation: Short-range, close-quarters communication between tug and tow uses the low-power (1 W) on-board channels 15 and 17.

items.prompt: What action do you take after a false alert transmitted on 406.025 MHz?

options.text: Send a DSC message on Channel 70 to cancel the false alert
Turn off the device and make a voice cancellation on Channel 16
Contact a nearby coast station and transmit a voice cancellation message
Turn off the NAVTEX and transmit a voice cancellation on Channel 12

items.explanation: A 406 MHz beacon alert reaches the satellite system, so cancel it by contacting a coast station (RCC) by voice/phone — not by VHF DSC.

items.prompt: To listen simultaneously on a Public Correspondence channel and the distress channel, you must:

options.text: Activate the Squelch function
Set the transceiver to receive in Duplex
Use the function called Dual Watch
Run the DSC and the VHF transceiver at the same time

items.explanation: Dual Watch alternately monitors a selected working channel and Ch16 so the distress channel is not left unguarded.

items.prompt: When you have finished communicating with a station/stations and expect no reply, you end with:

options.text: Over
Stand-By
Go Ahead
Out

items.explanation: OUT means the exchange is finished and no reply is expected. OVER invites a reply.

items.prompt: Which statement is correct regarding the NAVTEX device?

options.text: It operates in the S-Band
4209.5 kHz is one of its possible receive frequencies
It operates within a global MSI service
Answers b and c are correct

items.explanation: NAVTEX broadcasts on 518 kHz (international), 490 kHz (national language) and 4209.5 kHz (tropical/HF), as part of the worldwide MSI service.

items.prompt: The number 043805050 is:

options.text: A ship-station DSC MMSI
An MMSI belonging to a country with MID 043
An MMSI of a group of DSC stations
Answers b and c are correct

items.explanation: A single leading zero (0MIDxxxxx) marks a group-ship call; the digits after it are the MID, so this is a group of stations, not an individual ship or MID 043.

items.prompt: The Squelch function allows:

options.text: Cancelling interference from a nearby station
Cancelling the internal noise of the receiver system
Receiving only one station
Receiving a selected channel and Channel 16 simultaneously

items.explanation: (*) The exam keys 'a'. In real terms squelch mutes the receiver's background hiss/noise when no signal is present (option b is the technically accurate description); it is not an interference-from-a-station filter. Key 'a' preserved per policy.
items.note: (*) Squelch suppresses receiver background noise in the absence of a signal (option b), not interference from a nearby station. The exam keys 'a'; preserved per policy.

items.prompt: Choose the correct statement:

options.text: PRUDONCE means limited, careful working is possible on CH16
The word SEELONCE in distress means the end of the distress
PRUDONCE means silencing an interfering ship
SEELONCE FEENEE means silencing an interfering station by the controlling station

items.explanation: PRUDONCE relaxes radio silence to careful working on the distress channel while the distress is still in progress (not yet ended).

items.prompt: The range of NAVTEX:

options.text: Depends on the transmit and receive antenna heights
Is of the order of 20-25 nautical miles
Minimum 200, maximum 400 nautical miles
Minimum 400, maximum 800 nautical miles

items.explanation: NAVTEX on 518 kHz (MF ground wave) typically covers about 200-400 NM from the transmitter.

items.prompt: Choose the correct radio exchange:

options.text: Mayday Dorit x3 This is Sapir x3 Received Prudonce Out
Dorit x3 This is Sapir x3 Received Mayday Over
Mayday Dorit x3 This is Sapir x3 Seelonce Mayday Over
Mayday Dorit x3 This is Sapir x3 Received Mayday Over

items.explanation: Acknowledging a MAYDAY: "MAYDAY, [distressed vessel x3], THIS IS [own vessel x3], RECEIVED MAYDAY, OVER."

items.prompt: You are sailing the yacht IRIS and made a routine call to the vessel SAMSON. Which is correct?

options.text: IRIS controls the communication
SAMSON controls the communication; both vessels will work on channel CH08
SAMSON controls the communication; both vessels will work on channel CH13
The larger of the two vessels controls the communication, on a Duplex channel

items.explanation: The called station controls the exchange and nominates the working channel — here SAMSON, on intership Ch08.

items.prompt: The expression MAYDAY is transmitted when:

options.text: There is danger to the life of one or more persons and immediate assistance is needed
The transmitting station is not in distress but is relaying another vessel's distress call
Transmitting a distress alert via DSC on CH70
Answers a and c are correct

items.explanation: MAYDAY signals grave and imminent danger requiring immediate assistance. Relaying another's distress is MAYDAY RELAY; the DSC alert on Ch70 is a separate digital step.

items.prompt: The X-Band is intended for use by:

options.text: An emergency beacon (EPIRB) operating with COSPAS-SARSAT satellites
NAVTEX in the international language
A radar transponder (SART) for homing purposes
A beacon operating within the Inmarsat satellite system

items.explanation: The radar-SART operates in the X-Band (9 GHz) and is interrogated by ships' X-Band radars for homing in SAR.

items.prompt: In communications difficulty, how do you spell the group "RQ 2 RT CALLS"?

options.text: Require two RadioTelephone calls
Romeo Queen Two Romeo Tango Charlie Alfa Lima Sierra
Romeo Queen Bissotwo Tango Charlie Alfa Lima Lima Sierra
Romeo Quebec Bissotwo Romeo Tango Charlie Alfa Lima Lima Sierra

items.explanation: Each character is spelled with its ITU phonetic word: R=Romeo, Q=Quebec, 2=Bissotwo, R=Romeo, T=Tango, C=Charlie, A=Alfa, L=Lima, L=Lima, S=Sierra.

items.prompt: NAVTEX message PA21 was received garbled with an error rate CER=2%. You want to receive it again. What do you do?

options.text: Deleting the message code will cause it to be received again at the next transmission (in ~4 hours)
Contact the nearest coast station for the details of the garbled message
Contact the navy on channel 11 for the missing information
Nothing can be done; wait for the next transmission in ~4 hours

items.explanation: Because the receiver stores the message ID to avoid duplicates, deleting that stored ID lets the message be accepted and printed again at its next scheduled transmission.

items.prompt: Identify the coast station:

options.text: Eilat Radio
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: An MMSI beginning 00 (004560003) is a coast station, and a named '...Radio' (Eilat Radio) is a coast station — so both a and b identify coast stations.

items.prompt: What is the justification for cancelling a distress alert?

options.text: If the distress alert was a false alert transmitted via any radio beacon
If the distress alert was a false alert transmitted via the NAVTEX device
If the distress situation has ended and all stations must be informed
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: A false alert (e.g. from an accidentally triggered beacon) must be cancelled. NAVTEX is receive-only and cannot send alerts, so b is impossible.

items.prompt: Which statement is NOT correct regarding the MSI service?

options.text: The NAVTEX device provides automatic MSI in the MF and HF bands
In sea area A1 you rely on MSI produced via NAVTEX
In sea area A4 you rely on MSI produced via the Inmarsat satellite system
MSI messages received via NAVTEX are divided into 3 different priority levels

items.explanation: Inmarsat (geostationary) does not cover the polar Area A4, so relying on Inmarsat MSI there is incorrect — A4 MSI uses HF NBDP / the relevant high-latitude service.

items.prompt: The word CONTROL is used in communications relating to:

options.text: Controlling Distress communications
Controlling On-Scene communications
Bridge-to-Bridge communications
On Board Communication

items.explanation: In on-board (intra-ship) working the master/base set is addressed as "Control".

items.prompt: When a vessel receives a NAVTEX station about 300 NM away, you can say:

options.text: The wave was received by ground-wave propagation
The wave was received by space-wave propagation
The wave was received by sky-wave propagation
The wave was received by micro-wave propagation

items.explanation: NAVTEX on 518 kHz is in the MF band, which propagates as a ground wave to ranges of a few hundred nautical miles.

items.prompt: 003660033 calls 366428001 using emission class G2B. Which statement is correct?

options.text: A coast station is calling a ship station
The call is made via the DSC device
Both stations are of the same nationality
All the answers are correct

items.explanation: 003660033 (leading 00) is a coast station, 366428001 is a ship — both MID 366 (same nationality) — and G2B is the DSC emission designator, so all statements hold.

items.prompt: How are the first three letters of the alphabet pronounced phonetically?

options.text: Alfa Baker Choker
Alfa Baker Crystal
Alfa Bravo Charlie
Alfa Bravo Choker

items.explanation: The ITU/NATO phonetic words for A, B, C are Alfa, Bravo, Charlie.

items.prompt: After abandoning ship and being in the life raft, you should:

options.text: Place the SART high and let the EPIRB float in the water
Place the EPIRB high and throw the SART into the water
Not activate the EPIRB and SART unless a vessel is nearby
None of the answers is correct

items.explanation: Mount the SART as high as possible (its range is line-of-sight to radar); the EPIRB works floating in the water and should be activated.

items.prompt: An EPIRB can be received by the DSC system provided the beacon transmits on:

options.text: L-Band
Channel 70
The 406-406.1 MHz range
Channel 16

items.explanation: DSC operates on VHF Ch70; only a beacon transmitting a DSC signal on Ch70 could be received by a VHF DSC receiver.

items.prompt: Which frequency/channel suits space-wave (direct-wave) propagation?

options.text: CH12
518 kHz
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: VHF/UHF frequencies (121.5 MHz and Ch12) travel as a direct/space wave (line of sight); 518 kHz (MF) is a ground wave.

items.prompt: What are the communication priorities according to the Radio Regulations?

options.text: Vital, Important, Routine
Distress call, distress message, distress traffic
Activating DSC, activating EPIRB, activating SART
DISTRESS, URGENCY, SAFETY, SHIP'S BUSINESS, ROUTINE

items.explanation: The order of priority is Distress, Urgency, Safety, then ordinary (ship's business / routine) traffic.

items.prompt: What does this message mean? "Haifa Radio this is Tel-Aviv, TR Tel-Aviv left Haifa Bound Ashdod Over"

options.text: Tel-Aviv departed Haifa and will arrive at Ashdod
Haifa departed Tel-Aviv and will arrive at Ashdod
Ashdod departed Haifa and will arrive at Haifa
Tel-Aviv departed Ashdod and will arrive at Haifa

items.explanation: It is a transit report from the vessel Tel-Aviv: departed Haifa, bound for (arriving at) Ashdod.

items.prompt: Channel CH13 is used for communication:

options.text: Navigation safety between ships
With marinas worldwide
With the pilot when entering or leaving a port
None of the answers is correct

items.explanation: Ch13 is the bridge-to-bridge navigation-safety (ship-to-ship) channel.

items.prompt: Safety messages are:

options.text: Meteorological warnings
Navigation warnings
A vessel that is uncontrollable due to a malfunction
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: Safety (SECURITE) traffic covers meteorological and navigational warnings. A disabled vessel in danger would be urgency/distress.

items.prompt: State the MID of the station 234280482:
items.explanation: The MID is the first three digits of the MMSI — here 234.

items.prompt: In communications, to obtain a response from the other station, you say:

options.text: Out
Stand-by
Received
Over

items.explanation: OVER means "I have finished and expect a reply".

items.prompt: What is an abbreviated call on channel CH16?

options.text: A call in a dense-traffic area (where many coast and ship stations operate)
A call in which you call the called station once and identify yourself twice
A short distress call during abandonment
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: An abbreviated call names the called station once and gives "THIS IS [own identity]" twice, to keep Ch16 calling brief.

items.prompt: Which type of communication has the highest priority?

options.text: All types of messages relating to the NAVTEX device
All urgent messages
A transmission from an EPIRB unit operating in the VHF band
All communications carried out via the DSC device

items.explanation: (*) The exam keys 'd'. In real priority terms a distress beacon transmission (c) outranks ordinary DSC traffic; option d is questionable. Key 'd' preserved per policy.
items.note: (*) Questionable item: a distress beacon (c) should outrank generic DSC traffic. The exam keys 'd'; preserved per policy.

items.prompt: At the end of a NAVTEX message the indicator CER=6% appears. This means:

options.text: If the message is still valid it will be received again in about 4 hours
6% of the message's total characters are erroneous
The message ID code will not be stored in the device memory
All of the above

items.explanation: CER is the character error rate; above the threshold (~4%) the receiver does not store the message ID, so a still-valid message is reprinted at the next scheduled transmission.

items.prompt: An uncontrollable (not-under-command) vessel will request a tow by the following means:

options.text: Call the RCC and use SAFETY priority
Call the MCC and use URGENCY priority
Call All Stations and use second priority with the code PAN PAN
Request a tow from any vessel in the area and use URGENCY priority
Answers c and d are correct

items.explanation: A tow request for a disabled vessel not in grave danger is urgency (PAN PAN) to all stations. (*) Option d is also a valid urgency means, so the combined answer e is defensible; the exam keys c.
items.note: (*) Both c (PAN PAN All Stations) and d (urgency to any vessel) are valid, so e (c+d) is defensible. The exam keys c; preserved per policy.

items.prompt: What is the meaning of the NAVTEX message code PD00?

options.text: The message from station P will be received by all nearby receivers regardless of how the operator has programmed the device
Station D has no message but is declaring that it is operating
Station P has temporarily stopped operating
Station D has a Vital message for Area A1

items.explanation: Serial number 00 (B3B4=00) marks a message that bypasses the receiver's reject/suppress programming, so it is always printed.

items.prompt: The range of a NAVTEX station transmitting on 518 kHz will be:

options.text: Global coverage, since NAVTEX stations are spread along all coasts
Limited to between 70 degrees North and 70 degrees South
About 20-30 NM

items.explanation: A 518 kHz NAVTEX transmitter covers roughly 200-400 NM (MF ground wave).

items.prompt: What action do you take after a false alert from an Inmarsat-E type beacon?

options.text: Send a DSC message on Channel 70 to cancel
Make a voice cancellation and transmit it on Channel 16
Prepare a voice cancellation message and transmit it to a nearby coast station
Prepare a voice cancellation and transmit it to the main LES of the satellite region

items.explanation: A satellite beacon alert is cancelled by contacting a coast station / RCC to call off the response.

items.prompt: Which VHF radiotelephone working channel would you propose to the Istanbul coast station for connecting a call to a shore subscriber?

options.text: CH20
CH12
I will consult the List of Coast Stations to find the working channel, or alternatively the coast station will set the channel
CH16

items.explanation: A foreign coast station's public-correspondence working channels are found in the List of Coast Stations, or the coast station assigns one.

items.prompt: Channels 06 and 16 serve as:

options.text: An inter-ship channel and an internal channel
A search-and-rescue (SAR) channel and a distress channel
A Port Operation channel and a Ship Movements channel
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: Ch06 is used for on-scene/inter-ship SAR working and Ch16 is the distress/calling channel.

items.prompt: How often must the DSC be checked for serviceability during a voyage?

options.text: Once per voyage
In preparation for an authorized radio inspector's inspection
Once a day, recorded in the station log
At any opportunity that arises

items.explanation: The DSC is tested daily by internal means and the check is logged.

items.prompt: Why should batteries be tested under load?

options.text: To check the serviceability of the equipment connected to the batteries
To verify the batteries are actually fully charged; a no-load voltage reading can be misleading
To drain the batteries before each new charge
A no-load test endangers the integrity of the batteries

items.explanation: A battery can show a healthy open-circuit (no-load) voltage yet collapse under load; testing under load reveals its true state of charge/health.

items.prompt: The position of a beacon transmitting on 406.025 MHz is computed by:

options.text: The LES of the satellite region
The LUT
The RCC nearest the scene
The MCC, which passes the information to the RCC

items.explanation: The LUT (Local User Terminal) processes the satellite data to compute the beacon position, then forwards it via the MCC to the RCC.

items.prompt: Which channel do you use to pass a message longer than 2 minutes by radiotelephone to another ship?

options.text: CH26
CH87B
CH06
CH16

items.explanation: Move longer inter-ship traffic off Ch16 to an inter-ship working channel such as Ch06.

items.prompt: How does the search crew discover that a SART is transmitting and is in their immediate vicinity?

options.text: The SART dots on the radar screen begin to arc and finally become circles centred on its position
The DSC device is triggered by the SART signals and responds with an audible alarm
The SART can provide an approximate position within a radius of about 2 NM per the IMO standard
The SART signal appears as a target that appears and disappears

items.explanation: At close range the 12 SART response dots widen into arcs and then concentric circles centred on the SART, indicating it is very near.

items.prompt: What does T.L. mean in communication procedures?

options.text: Passage details
Traffic lists, from a coast station to ships
Telephone
A dangerous list/heel of the vessel

items.explanation: T.L. is the traffic list — a coast station's periodic list of vessels for which it holds messages.

items.prompt: On the following channels you speak only in SIMPLEX:

options.text: CH16, CH06
CH26, CH27
CH24, CH25
Answers b and c are correct

items.explanation: Ch16 and Ch06 are simplex (single-frequency) channels; Ch24/25/26/27 are duplex public-correspondence channels.

items.prompt: The number 231453432 is:

options.text: A ship-station DSC MMSI belonging to a country with MID 314
A ship-station DSC MMSI belonging to a country with MID 432
A ship-station DSC MMSI belonging to a country with MID 231
A ship-station DSC MMSI belonging to a country with MID 343

items.explanation: The MID is the first three digits of the MMSI — here 231.

items.prompt: Which statement is correct regarding a COSPAS-SARSAT type beacon?

options.text: It operates in the L-Band
It operates in the X-Band
It uses the Doppler effect
It operates in the 156-174 MHz band

items.explanation: COSPAS-SARSAT determines a 406 MHz beacon's position using the Doppler shift measured by low-earth-orbit satellites (plus encoded GPS where available).

items.prompt: What is the meaning of On Board Communication?

options.text: Two VHF transceivers linked together that are located on the same vessel
Search-and-rescue communication
Emergency installations that operate automatically during distress
Communication channels for AIS purposes

items.explanation: On-board communication is intra-ship working between handheld/fixed VHF sets on the same vessel (low power, Ch15/17).

items.prompt: A lighthouse has stopped working; consequently a message will be transmitted of type:

options.text: DISTRESS, to warn of a hazard dangerous to vessels
URGENCY, to warn that vessels without lighthouse marking are effectively not under command
SAFETY, to warn of a danger to the safety of navigation
ROUTINE, to pass a routine message about a coastal installation being out of service

items.explanation: An unlit/failed lighthouse is a navigational hazard, broadcast as a SECURITE (safety) navigation warning.

items.prompt: Distress messages are:

options.text: Meteorological warnings
Navigation warnings
Man overboard
Injured person
Answers c and d are correct

items.explanation: Man overboard is a distress situation. (*) An injured person (d) may be distress or urgency depending on severity, so e is arguable; the exam keys c (man overboard).
items.note: (*) Man overboard is distress; an injured person is usually urgency (medical) unless life-threatening, making e arguable. The exam keys c; preserved per policy.

items.prompt: Which statement is NOT correct regarding the handheld survival-craft VHF?

options.text: It operates in Simplex on 156.525 MHz (Channel 70) and at least one additional channel
It is waterproof to a depth of 1 m for at least 5 minutes
Its minimum effective transmit power is 0.25 Watt
All of the above

items.explanation: Survival-craft portable VHF works on Ch16 plus working channels — not Ch70 (which is DSC) — so statement a is the incorrect one.

items.prompt: Who would transmit the following message: "Dalia SEELONCE MAYDAY"?

options.text: A coast station controlling the distress
A vessel in distress (which is controlling the distress) silencing the station Dalia
Dalia is the vessel in distress, silencing all interfering stations
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: "[name] SEELONCE MAYDAY" imposes silence on the named station; it is sent by whoever is controlling the distress — either the distressed vessel or a coast station.

items.prompt: What does DSC stand for?

options.text: Direct Selective Command
A device for transmitting and receiving distress homing signals
Duplex Selective Calling
Digital Selective Calling

items.explanation: DSC = Digital Selective Calling.

items.prompt: What is the justification for cancelling a distress alert?

options.text: If the distress alert was a false alert transmitted via the SART / NAVTEX
If the distress alert was a false alert transmitted via the DSC device
If the distress situation has ended and the stations must be informed
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: A false DSC alert must be cancelled. The SART/NAVTEX cannot originate a distress alert, so option a is impossible.

items.prompt: Which expression is correct for use in urgency traffic?

options.text: SEELONCE PANPAN
PAN PAN ALL STATIONS
PRUDONCE MEDICO
MEDICO SECURITE

items.explanation: An urgency announcement is "PAN PAN (x3), ALL STATIONS (x3) ..." (or a named station).

items.prompt: What represents a device for receiving Maritime Safety Information?

options.text: The DSC system, using the MMSI code
A radar receiving a SART
A NAVTEX receiver tuned to receive on 518 kHz
All of the answers are correct

items.explanation: NAVTEX (518 kHz) is the dedicated automatic MSI receiver.

items.prompt: You made a routine call to a coast station on CH70 and received no reply. You will call the coast station again after:

options.text: 5 minutes
5 seconds
2 minutes
3 minutes

items.explanation: Allow an interval (about 5 minutes) before repeating a call to avoid congesting the calling channel.

items.prompt: The possible meaning of the group PC21:

options.text: A DSC alert identity code
A NAVTEX message identity code
An official identity sign of a satellite beacon
Message no. 21 relating to Public Correspondence communication

items.explanation: PC21 is a NAVTEX technical code: P = transmitter station, C = subject indicator (ice reports), 21 = serial number.

items.prompt: The distance between a NAVTEX-D transmitter in NAVAREA 3 and a NAVTEX-D transmitter in NAVAREA 2 will be:

options.text: At least 400 NM
At least 800 NM
Between 200 NM and 400 NM
The distance is unimportant because of time-sharing operation

items.explanation: Two transmitters sharing the same B1 character (D) must be separated enough to avoid co-channel interference given the ~200-400 NM range — on the order of 800 NM apart.

items.prompt: What prompts the need to transmit a distress relay (Mayday Relay) via the NAVTEX network?

options.text: It is the only means by which the RCC can communicate with vessels in the area
It is a means to minimise DSC communication congestion during distress
Extending the search circle for survivors beyond the range of VHF communication
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: Broadcasting the relay via NAVTEX reaches vessels far beyond VHF range, widening the pool of potential rescuers.

items.prompt: Correct phonetic spelling of the group PUB30:

options.text: PAPA UNA BRAVA TERRATHREE ZERO
PENTA UNA BRAVO THREE ZERO
PAPA UNIFORM BRAVO TERRATHREE NADAZERO
PAPAPENTA UNA BRAVO TERRATHREE NADAZERO

items.explanation: P=Papa, U=Uniform, B=Bravo, 3=Terrathree, 0=Nadazero (ITU figure pronunciation).

items.prompt: The following port-services communication channel is a first-priority Simplex channel:

options.text: CH10
CH20
CH60
CH12

items.explanation: Ch12 is a port-operations (port-services) simplex channel.

items.prompt: Choose the correct statement (battery check):

options.text: To check the batteries, switch off all station equipment and then check their terminal voltage
To check the batteries, switch on all station equipment and then check the specific gravity of each cell
To check the batteries, operate all station equipment (VHF transmitting) and then check their terminal voltage
It is recommended to fast-charge the batteries at high power continuously, to extend their life

items.explanation: Test the batteries under load (equipment on, VHF transmitting) and read the terminal voltage — a no-load reading can hide a weak battery.

items.prompt: Which of the following would you transmit at first (highest) priority?

options.text: A message about the end of a distress situation
A pirate attack
Man overboard
All of the answers are correct

items.explanation: All three concern distress-level traffic (grave and imminent danger / distress handling), so all are first-priority.

items.prompt: Channel CH13 is used for communication:

options.text: Navigation safety between a coast station and a vessel
With marinas worldwide
With the pilot when entering or leaving a port
None of the answers is correct

items.explanation: Ch13 is bridge-to-bridge navigation safety between ships — not coast-to-ship, not marinas; the exam treats the listed options as not matching that, so 'none'.

items.prompt: Safety messages are:

options.text: A warning of a snowstorm/blizzard
A message about a lighthouse that is not working
An engine breakdown of a vessel on the high seas
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: Safety (SECURITE) covers meteorological warnings (blizzard) and navigational warnings (unlit lighthouse). A disabled vessel in danger is urgency/distress.

items.prompt: Which of the following combinations indicates an MMSI of a group of vessels?
items.explanation: A group-ship MMSI is 9 digits with a single leading zero (0MIDxxxxx); only 036611111 fits that form.

items.prompt: Two stations established communication on channel 25:

options.text: The communication is for AIS purposes
The communication is between a ship station and a coast station
The communication is between two ship stations
The communication is between the port authority and a ship station

items.explanation: Ch25 is a duplex public-correspondence channel, used between a ship and a coast station.

items.prompt: The expression PAN PAN means:

options.text: A message concerning the illness or injury of a crew member
An urgency message requesting second priority in communications
A distress message requesting first priority in communications
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: PAN PAN is urgency (second priority) and is exactly what is used for a crew illness/injury, so both a and b apply.

items.prompt: The electromagnetic waves radiated by the SART propagate according to the principles of:

options.text: Sky-wave propagation
Ground-wave propagation
Space-wave (direct-wave) propagation
Digital-wave propagation

items.explanation: The SART works at 9 GHz (X-Band), which propagates as a direct/space wave (line of sight).

items.prompt: Which of the following identity signs identifies a coast station?

options.text: Rijeka Radio
Haifa
Answers a + c are correct
Answers a + b are correct

items.explanation: An MMSI beginning 00 (004280001) is a coast station, and a named '...Radio' (Rijeka Radio) is a coast station; 'Haifa' alone is not a station identity.

items.prompt: During your watch you heard SECURITE spoken 3 times on CH16. The meaning is:

options.text: A coast station is announcing the danger of a storm
A coast station is announcing the presence of a sea mine in the area
A mobile unit needs a tow
Answers a + b are correct
Answers b + c are correct

items.explanation: SECURITE precedes safety warnings — both a storm warning (meteorological) and a sea-mine warning (navigational) qualify.

items.prompt: You are on a vessel in distress and want to communicate with a search aircraft circling overhead. Which channel do you use?

options.text: CH70
CH06
CH13
CH12

items.explanation: Ch06 is the on-scene inter-unit channel used with SAR aircraft.

items.prompt: What information does the NAVTEX system provide?

options.text: Maritime Safety Information
Navigation warnings, meteorological warnings and SAR-related information
Distress communication and SAR information between ships
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: NAVTEX delivers MSI: navigational and meteorological warnings and SAR information.

items.prompt: The SART will operate in receive-only mode when:

options.text: It is thrown in the water and starts working automatically
It is manually activated and an X-Band radar is operating in the vicinity
It is manually activated and there is no X-Band radar operating in the vicinity
The SART only transmits

items.explanation: Once armed, the SART listens; it only transmits a response when an X-Band radar interrogates it — with no radar nearby it stays in receive (standby).

items.prompt: The international radiotelephone distress call comprises:

options.text: DISTRESS x3 THIS IS NAME x3
DISTRESS x3 THIS IS CALL/SIGN x3
MAYDAY x3 THIS IS NAME x3
MAYDAY ALL STATIONS x3 THIS IS NAME x3

items.explanation: The distress call is "MAYDAY (x3), THIS IS [vessel name] (x3)".

items.prompt: Transmission of a distress message by a station that is not itself in distress is carried out:

options.text: When the vessel in distress is not in a position to transmit a distress itself
When the master or responsible person on a vessel not in distress decides to
When the responsible person at a coast station believes further help is needed
Each of the preceding situations can justify transmitting a distress relay

items.explanation: A MAYDAY RELAY may be sent in any of those circumstances.

items.prompt: The vessel ALBERTA called your vessel on channel 16. Who decides the working channel to continue on?

options.text: The vessel closest to shore
The vessel ALBERTA
Your vessel
The smaller vessel, because it has fewer communication means

items.explanation: The called station (yours) controls the exchange and nominates the working channel.

items.prompt: The station called LUT is a communication link operating in a system related to:

options.text: MSI
Automatic Identification System
Locating and identifying a radio beacon
Global Positioning System

items.explanation: The LUT (Local User Terminal) is part of COSPAS-SARSAT, processing satellite data to locate and identify distress beacons.

items.prompt: Which of the following cases is appropriate for urgency?

options.text: A woman giving birth aboard the vessel who needs assistance from shore
A warning of a dangerous tropical storm
Pirates trying to take over the vessel
Answers a and c are correct

items.explanation: Childbirth needing shore medical assistance is urgency (PAN PAN). A storm warning is safety; a piracy takeover is normally a distress.

items.prompt: Bridge-to-Bridge communication is conducted on channel(s):

options.text: CH15 and CH17
CH70
CH13
CH11 and CH79

items.explanation: Ch13 is the bridge-to-bridge navigation-safety channel.

items.prompt: Which VHF radiotelephone working channel would you propose to Ashdod port for communication with the port authorities?

options.text: CH13
CH14
CH26
CH22

items.explanation: Local assignment: port-operations working with Ashdod port control is on Ch14.

items.prompt: What does this message on CH16 mean? "All Ships x3 this is Haifa Radio x3 Navigation Warning CH25"

options.text: Haifa coast station has important safety traffic
Haifa coast station has traffic for several vessels, announced via traffic lists transmitted on Ch25
Haifa coast station warns vessels not to transmit on Ch25
No answer is correct

items.explanation: The station is announcing it will broadcast a navigation warning (safety traffic) on Ch25.

items.prompt: What do the frequencies/channels 156.8 MHz, 121.5 MHz and CH06 have in common?

options.text: Channels that work together with the COSPAS-SARSAT system
Emergency channels during search and rescue in which aircraft also participate
Channels used for homing during search and rescue
There is no common denominator for these frequencies/channels

items.explanation: 156.8 MHz (Ch16), 121.5 MHz (aeronautical emergency) and Ch06 are SAR communication frequencies usable with participating aircraft.

items.prompt: The frequency 518 kHz is intended for use:

options.text: An emergency beacon (EPIRB) operating with COSPAS-SARSAT satellites
Transmitting NAVTEX messages in the international language
Transmitting within the Maritime Safety Information service
Answers b and c are correct

items.explanation: 518 kHz carries international-language NAVTEX, which is part of the MSI service.

items.prompt: Which statement is correct regarding the DSC device?

options.text: It operates in Simplex on channel 70 and at least one additional channel
It decodes and encodes digital transmissions in emission class G2B
Its maximum effective transmit power is 25 Watt
All of the above

items.explanation: VHF DSC works simplex on Ch70 (plus working channels), uses emission G2B, at up to 25 W — all the statements hold.

items.prompt: To whom can the RCC transmit a Distress Relay Alert?

options.text: To all stations
To a group of ships or a specific ship
To a defined area
Answers a + b are correct
Answers a, b and c are correct

items.explanation: A distress relay alert can be addressed to all stations, to a selective group/individual, or to ships in a defined geographic area.

items.prompt: During a voyage at sea, a transmit/receive test of the survival-craft portable VHF should be carried out at the following interval:

options.text: Once a day
Once a week
Once a month
Once per voyage

items.explanation: The survival-craft (two-way) VHF is tested weekly (without transmitting on Ch16).

items.prompt: VHF channel 70 is used for the following purposes:

options.text: A DSC channel for distress, urgency, safety and calling alerts
A channel that replaces VHF Ch16 for routine communication when Ch16 is busy
Used to announce the cancellation of distress situations
Answers a, b and c are correct

items.explanation: Ch70 is the VHF DSC channel for distress, urgency, safety and routine digital calling — it is not a voice channel.

items.prompt: What is an abbreviated call on VHF channel 16?

options.text: You call the station once and identify yourself twice
You call the station twice and identify yourself once
You call the station twice and identify yourself twice
A call using the DSC method

items.explanation: An abbreviated call names the called station once and gives "THIS IS [own identity]" twice.

items.prompt: The sea-area coverage of Area A1 in the GMDSS is:

options.text: A sea coverage of 200-400 nautical miles from the coast
A sea coverage of 60 miles from the coast
The VHF (VHF) coverage of at least one coast station
Each state may decide the size of the Area A1 coverage

items.explanation: Area A1 is the area within VHF range of at least one shore station providing DSC alerting (typically ~20-30 NM).

items.prompt: The NAVTEX message safety levels are divided in the following order of priority:

options.text: Vital, Important, Routine
Important, Vital, Routine
Routine, Vital, Important
Vital, Routine, Important

items.explanation: The order is Vital (most urgent), then Important, then Routine.

items.prompt: A station in distress wishing to silence a station or stations interfering with its distress will use the words:

options.text: MAYDAY SEELONCE
MAYDAY DISTRESS
SEELONCE MAYDAY
SEELONCE DISTRESS

items.explanation: The distress-controlling station imposes silence with "SEELONCE MAYDAY".

items.prompt: How do you end an urgency situation that you broadcast to all stations in the maritime service?

options.text: End it with a message on DSC
End it with a message on VHF channel 16
End it with a DSC call followed by a message on VHF channel 16
There is no obligation to announce the end of an urgency situation

items.explanation: Cancel an all-stations PAN PAN with a voice message on Ch16 telling stations the urgency traffic is finished.

items.prompt: The SART battery is sufficient to operate the device in receive (STAND BY) mode for:

options.text: Eight hours
Forty-eight hours
Seventy-two hours
Ninety-six hours

items.explanation: A SART provides 96 hours in standby, plus several hours of responding when interrogated.

items.prompt: The transmission range of most NAVTEX stations is:

options.text: Usually 50-100 nautical miles
Usually over 1000 miles during daytime
Limited to about 30 miles from the coast
Usually 200-400 nautical miles

items.explanation: Most NAVTEX stations cover about 200-400 NM.

items.prompt: The following VHF channels are used as inter-ship (INTERSHIP) communication channels:

options.text: CH12 and CH06
CH12 and CH08
CH06 and CH08
CH14 and CH12

items.explanation: Ch06 and Ch08 are inter-ship channels; Ch12/14 are port-operations channels.

items.prompt: What does Dual Watch mean in maritime communication procedures?

options.text: Simultaneous reception of channel 16 and a selected working channel
Simultaneous reception of two working frequencies of two different stations
Listening alternately to channel 16 and a selected working frequency
Simultaneous transmission on channel 16 and a selected working frequency

items.explanation: Dual Watch monitors the distress/calling channel (Ch16) and a selected working channel by listening to them alternately, so Ch16 is not left unguarded.

items.prompt: The group UN2MO in the international phonetic alphabet is:

options.text: UNION NOVEMBER TWOBISSO MIKE OSLO
UNIFORM NOVEMBER BISSOTWO MIKE OSCAR
UNIFORM NOVEMBER TWOBISSO MIKE OSCAR
UNION NOVEMBER BISSOTWO MIKE OSLO

items.explanation: U=Uniform, N=November, 2=Bissotwo, M=Mike, O=Oscar.

items.prompt: An MMSI of a group of vessels would be:
items.explanation: A group-ship MMSI is 9 digits with a single leading zero (0MIDxxxxx) — 024700222 (MID 247).

items.prompt: Regarding VHF channel 15:

options.text: It is a channel intended for ship safety only
There is a power limit of one watt on this channel
It is a channel intended for vessel-traffic control
It is a channel intended for public traffic

items.explanation: Ch15 is limited to 1 W and used for on-board communications.

items.prompt: Which of the following cases is appropriate for safety (SAFETY) messages?

options.text: A person overboard
A request for medical assistance
A message about an approaching storm in the Middle East
All the answers are correct

items.explanation: An approaching-storm (meteorological) warning is safety traffic. Man overboard is distress; medical assistance is urgency.

items.prompt: A coast station received your distress alert transmitted via DSC. On which channel will the coast station acknowledge receipt of the distress alert?

options.text: Channel 6
Channel 13
Channel 16
Channel 70

items.explanation: A coast station typically acknowledges a VHF DSC distress alert by voice on Ch16 (after, or instead of, a DSC acknowledgement).

items.prompt: The term PRUDONCE indicates that:

options.text: The distress situation has ended
The distress situation has not yet ended and all communication is forbidden except distress communication
Careful working is permitted on the distress channel even though the distress situation has not yet ended
Only stations taking part in the distress traffic may operate on the distress channel

items.explanation: PRUDONCE relaxes full radio silence to careful working while the distress is still in progress.

items.prompt: In ordinary radio communication between a ship station and a coast station, the station controlling the communication is:

options.text: The calling station
The called station
The ship station
The coast station

items.explanation: In communication between a ship station and a coast station, the coast station is the controlling station.

items.prompt: The primary purpose of the GMDSS is:

options.text: Efficient and inexpensive general communication
To automate and improve emergency communication in world shipping
To provide more means for efficient communication with the shipping company
To allow more efficient control of SAR situations by vessels

items.explanation: GMDSS exists to automate and improve distress and safety communications for shipping worldwide.

items.prompt: A marine radar transponder operates in the band:

options.text: S-Band
X-Band
L-Band
C-Band

items.explanation: A radar-SART operates in the X-Band (9 GHz), the band of ships' navigational radars.

items.prompt: By what means can a coast station extend the circle of assistance to a vessel in distress beyond Area A1?

options.text: Ask all vessels via DSC to transmit a DISTRESS RELAY
Transmit a Vital NAVTEX message
Transmit its own distress call and message via VHF DSC
Answers b and c are correct

items.explanation: NAVTEX reaches well beyond VHF range (~200-400 NM), so a Vital NAVTEX broadcast extends the call for assistance beyond Area A1; VHF DSC (c) stays within A1.

items.prompt: Which communication system provides data for preventing collisions in dense-traffic areas?

options.text: AIS
COSPAS-SARSAT
EPIRB
NAVTEX

items.explanation: AIS exchanges identity, position, course and speed between vessels, building a traffic picture used to avoid collisions.

items.prompt: When will the NAVTEX device reject a particular message?

options.text: When the serial number and message type from a particular station have already been received
When the message arrived with an error rate above 5%
When the message identity code ends with the digits 00
When the message type relates to a message coded with the letter Z

items.explanation: To avoid printing duplicates, the receiver rejects a message whose station + subject + serial number it has already stored.

items.prompt: Which system provides accurate position information to the GMDSS?

options.text: GPS
COSPAS-SARSAT
EPIRB
GEO

items.explanation: GPS supplies the accurate position that GMDSS equipment (DSC, EPIRB) embeds in distress alerts.

items.prompt: The frequency 490 kHz is intended for use:

options.text: An emergency beacon (EPIRB) operating with COSPAS-SARSAT satellites
Transmitting NAVTEX messages in the national language
Transmitting NAVTEX messages in the English language
This frequency is not used in the GMDSS

items.explanation: 490 kHz carries NAVTEX in the national language; 518 kHz is the international (English) NAVTEX frequency.

items.prompt: What are the justifications for sending passage details (a TR) to a coast station?

options.text: More efficient routing of EPIRB alerts of all kinds
Improving search-and-rescue performance
Standing orders of the navy to improve security
Improving traffic routing

items.explanation: (*) The exam keys 'd' (traffic routing). Note that improving SAR performance (b) is also a standard reason for sending a TR; this paper keys traffic routing.
items.note: (*) Improving SAR performance (b) is also a primary, widely-taught reason for a TR; the exam keys 'd' (traffic routing). Preserved per policy.

items.prompt: Identify the coast station:

options.text: HAIFA
Dakar Radio
Answers b and c are correct

items.explanation: A coast station is identified either by the convention '<place> Radio' (Dakar Radio — the coast radio station at Dakar) or by a 9-digit MMSI beginning 00 (005673333). 'HAIFA' on its own is just a place name, not a station identity — so b and c are both coast stations.

items.prompt: At sea, all equipment (other than survival-craft equipment) requires proof of serviceability by:

options.text: A daily check
Either a or c
Routine operation of the equipment
A check at least every 48 hours

items.explanation: Serviceability is shown either by the daily functional check or by the equipment being used routinely.

items.prompt: The group DSC20 spelled phonetically would be:

options.text: DIGITAL SENSOR CALL BISSOTWO NADAZERO
DELTA SENSOR CHARLIE BISSOTOW NADAZERO
DOLPHIN SIERRA CHARLIE BISSOTWO NADAZERO
DELTA SIERRA CHARLIE BISSOTWO NADAZERO

items.explanation: D=Delta, S=Sierra, C=Charlie, 2=Bissotwo, 0=Nadazero.

items.prompt: Which equipment is used in or near a survival craft?

options.text: NAVTEX
A depth sounder
DSC
EPIRB
