items.explanation: The EPIRB (with the SART and portable VHF) is survival-craft/abandonment equipment.

items.prompt: What does this message on Channel 16 mean? "All Stations x3 This is Rota Radio x3 Gale Warning on Channel 27"

options.text: Rota Radio announces it has a gale warning and will detail it on CH27
A ship station asks Rota Radio whether it has a gale warning on CH27
Rota Radio asks all stations whether they have information about a gale warning
Rota Radio is ready to receive a gale warning from vessels on CH27

items.explanation: The coast station announces a safety (gale) warning on Ch16 and gives the working channel (Ch27) where it will broadcast it.

items.prompt: The use of CH16 is:

options.text: A distress, urgency, safety and calling channel by radiotelephone
A DSC channel for distress, urgency and safety alerts
A channel that replaces CH70 in the GMDSS
A channel used for broadcasting navigation messages and weather reports

items.explanation: Ch16 is the VHF voice (radiotelephone) distress, urgency, safety and calling channel; Ch70 is the DSC channel.

items.prompt: Seaweed calls Damien in Area A1, within a coast station's coverage. Which station controls the traffic?

options.text: Damien
Seaweed
The coast station in the area
Answers b and c are correct

items.explanation: The called station (Damien) controls a ship-to-ship exchange.

items.prompt: The term Bridge to Bridge communication is used for:

options.text: Communication during a distress event
Internal communication
A channel between ships for navigation safety
A communication channel between vessels

items.explanation: Bridge-to-bridge is the navigation-safety channel (Ch13) between ships.

items.prompt: What is safety communication?

options.text: A radiotelephone warning preceded by the word PAN PAN
Traffic concerning the health, welfare and protection of a seafarer
Any transmission indicating that a station is about to transmit an important navigation or weather warning
All the answers are correct

items.explanation: Safety (SECURITE) traffic announces important navigational or meteorological warnings.

items.prompt: Which message-type symbols cannot be rejected or suppressed by the programmer of the NAVTEX device?

options.text: A, B, D, Z
A, B, D, L
A, B, D, L, E
A, B, D, E

items.explanation: Subject indicators A (navigational warnings), B (meteorological warnings), D (SAR information) and L (additional navigational warnings) cannot be suppressed by the operator.

items.prompt: Transmitting a Transit Report requires the priority:

options.text: URGENCY
DISTRESS
ROUTINE
SAFETY

items.explanation: A transit/traffic report is ordinary (routine) traffic.

items.prompt: The term SBM means:

options.text: Simplex Bi-directional Mode
Sea Basic Maintenance
Shore Based Maintenance
Sea Born Maintenance

items.explanation: SBM = Shore Based Maintenance, one of the accepted means of ensuring equipment availability.

items.prompt: Which of the following identities is NOT a coast station?

options.text: Vigo Radio
4XO
Haifa

items.explanation: 003661234 (leading 00) and Vigo Radio are coast stations; 'Haifa' alone is just a place name, not a station identity.

items.prompt: In which publication do the medical codes appear?

options.text: ITU List of Coast Stations
GMDSS Radio Logbook
International Code of Signals
No correct answer

items.explanation: The medical section/codes for MEDICO advice are in the International Code of Signals.

items.prompt: Which statement is NOT correct regarding a GPIRB?

options.text: A minimum of 4 GEO satellites is required for global coverage (70N-70S)
The position of the beacon is determined by Doppler shift
Availability for reception is up to 90 minutes when used above 70 degrees latitude
Position accuracy depends on the GPS accuracy at the moment of activation

items.explanation: A GPIRB's position comes from its built-in GPS, not from Doppler shift, so statement b is the incorrect one.

items.prompt: Which of the following channels/frequencies provide a homing capability for SAR?

options.text: CH06
9 GHz
Answers b and d are correct
Answers b and c are correct

items.explanation: Homing is provided by the 121.5 MHz beacon signal and by the 9 GHz (X-Band) SART; 406.025 MHz carries the alert data, not a homing signal.

items.prompt: All transmissions on CH16 (other than distress) are limited to:

options.text: A minimum
A minimum and not exceeding one minute
A minimum and not exceeding 10 seconds
As long as required

items.explanation: Calling on Ch16 must be kept to a minimum and should not exceed one minute.

items.prompt: Choose the correct channels designated for public correspondence with coast stations:
items.explanation: Channels 24-28 are duplex public-correspondence channels for shore-link calls via coast stations.

items.prompt: By which means would you initiate a distress alert while on a vessel in Area A1?

options.text: VHF CH16
DSC CH70
GPIRB
SART
AIS
Answers b and c are correct

items.explanation: A distress alert is initiated by DSC on Ch70 and/or the (G)EPIRB; Ch16 voice is the follow-up call, and the SART/AIS are locating aids, not alerting means.

items.prompt: What are the uses of the portable VHF?

options.text: Communication between a ship's survival craft and the mother ship
Communication between survival craft and ships or rescue units
For internal communication on appropriate channels and power levels
All the answers are correct

items.explanation: A portable VHF serves survival-craft-to-ship, survival-craft-to-rescue-unit, and (on appropriate channels/power) on-board communication.

items.prompt: Which of the following are considered AIS data?

options.text: Routine data, Important data, Vital data
Static data, Dynamic data, Voyage data
Urgent data, Non-urgent data, Safety data
None of these are AIS data

items.explanation: AIS transmits Static (identity/dimensions), Dynamic (position/course/speed) and Voyage-related data.

items.prompt: By which means can we receive a distress relay on a vessel in Area A1?

options.text: By a voice relay on CH16 from a vessel or coast station, by DSC, and by NAVTEX
By DSC, by NAVTEX, by SART
By DSC, by NAVTEX, by AIS
By DSC, by NAVTEX, by EPIRB/GPIRB

items.explanation: A relay can reach you as a voice relay on Ch16, as a DSC relay, or as a NAVTEX broadcast; SART/AIS/EPIRB are not relay-receiving means.

items.prompt: What are the main uses of CH06 on VHF?

options.text: Exchange of information between a vessel and aircraft in a SAR area
Initiating a distress alert by the vessel in distress
Exchange of information and public traffic between ship and ship (Intership)
Receiving MSI, if preceded by an announcement on CH16 by the coast station
Answers a and c are correct

items.explanation: Ch06 is used for ship-aircraft SAR information exchange and for inter-ship communication.

items.prompt: When a distress alert occurs on DSC (CH70), stations within DSC range will receive the following details:

options.text: MMSI of the vessel in distress, nature of distress, time and position, call priority
MMSI of the vessel, nature, time and position, call priority, ship's name
Name of the vessel in distress, nature, time and position, call priority
MMSI of the vessel, ship's name, time and position, call priority

items.explanation: A DSC distress alert carries the MMSI (not the name), the nature of distress, the position and time, and the distress priority.

items.prompt: Announcing the transmission of a safety message when using DSC requires switching to which working channel?

options.text: CH16
CH06
CH13

items.explanation: A DSC safety announcement directs stations to Ch16 (or a stated working channel) for the voice safety message.

items.prompt: What is the meaning of the term MCC?

options.text: Maritime Controlling Centre
Mission Control Centre
Marine Coordinating Commander
Marine Coastal Crew

items.explanation: MCC = Mission Control Centre, which receives beacon data from the LUT and forwards it to the appropriate RCC.

items.prompt: Among the following identity signs, identify the coast station:

options.text: Roma Radio
Haifa
Answers a + b are correct

items.explanation: An MMSI beginning 00 (002130001) and a named '...Radio' (Roma Radio) are both coast stations; 'Haifa' alone is not a station identity.

items.prompt: During your watch you heard PRUDONCE said 3 times. The meaning is:

options.text: A navigation warning or meteorological warning will follow
A station is preparing to transmit a false-alert cancellation
A mobile unit needs immediate help
No correct answer

items.explanation: PRUDONCE is used by the distress-controlling station to allow careful working; it is not an announced-three-times prefix like the ones described, so none of the options is correct.

items.prompt: Which maritime devices can be homed on during search and rescue?

options.text: NAVTEX + AIS
EPIRB + SART
DSC
NAVTEX + DSC

items.explanation: SAR units home on the EPIRB (121.5 MHz) and the SART (9 GHz radar response).

items.prompt: In a distress alert, the DSC will automatically transmit the alert at a cycle of:

options.text: Every 3.5-4.5 minutes
2 minutes, 2 minutes, 3 minutes, and so on
5 minutes and then 15 minutes
Once every 90 minutes

items.explanation: An unacknowledged DSC distress alert is repeated automatically at random intervals of 3.5-4.5 minutes until acknowledged.

items.prompt: You are listening and heard a distress false-alert cancellation from a ship station near you:

options.text: Contact the station that transmitted the cancellation and acknowledge receipt
Record the false-alert cancellation in the station log and continue with the routine
Relay this message to a nearby coast station
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: A cancellation only needs to be logged; no acknowledgement or relay is required.

items.prompt: Which of the following portable devices is fitted with a lithium battery intended to provide at least 96 hours of operation?

options.text: EPIRB
Portable VHF
SART
GPIRB

items.explanation: The SART provides at least 96 hours in standby on its lithium battery.

items.prompt: Your vessel is disabled (not under command) but not in clear and immediate danger. What do you do?

options.text: Call a coast station and request immediate evacuation
Broadcast a tow request on Channel 16 addressed to all stations
Any loss-of-control situation requires transmitting a distress alert
All the answers are correct

items.explanation: Not in grave danger, so it is an urgency (PAN PAN) tow request to all stations on Ch16 — not a distress.

items.prompt: The AIS device works in combination with the:

options.text: Navtex
DSC
VHF transceiver
EPIRB

items.explanation: (*) The exam keys 'b' (DSC). In real terms AIS is a VHF data system that works with the VHF transceiver and GPS (option c is the technically apt one). Key 'b' preserved per policy.
items.note: (*) AIS is a VHF data broadcast (works with the VHF transceiver/GPS), not DSC. The exam keys 'b'; preserved per policy.

items.prompt: The frequency 518 kHz is intended for use:

options.text: An emergency beacon (EPIRB) operating with COSPAS-SARSAT satellites
Transmitting NAVTEX messages in the international language
The new frequency of the GPIRB
The AIS system

items.explanation: 518 kHz carries international-language (English) NAVTEX.

items.prompt: Which of the following devices operates in the X-Band?

options.text: NAVTEX
AIS
SART
GPIRB

items.explanation: The radar-SART operates in the X-Band (9 GHz).

items.prompt: To whom can the RCC transmit a Distress Relay Alert?

options.text: To all stations
To a group of ships or a specific ship
To a defined area
Answers a, b and c are correct

items.explanation: A distress relay alert can be addressed to all stations, to a selective group/individual, or to a defined geographic area.

items.prompt: A radiotelephone-equipped ship keeps a watch on Channel 70 on the high seas:

options.text: Whenever there is no crew member next to the VHF set
16 hours a day
24 hours a day
8 hours a day

items.explanation: The DSC watch on Ch70 is kept continuously (24 hours) by the dedicated DSC watch receiver.

items.prompt: The expression PAN PAN means:

options.text: A message related to a warning of a fire drill
An urgency message requesting second priority in communications
A distress message requesting first priority in communications
Answers a and b are correct

items.explanation: PAN PAN is the urgency signal — second priority after distress.

items.prompt: In which publication would you find the name of the vessel with the code 227888022?

options.text: Admiralty VOL.5
List of Call Signs and Numerical Identities
Admiralty VOL.9
List of Radio Determination and Special Service Stations

items.explanation: An MMSI/numerical identity is looked up in the List of Call Signs and Numerical Identities.

items.prompt: What is a hydrometer?

options.text: A device that measures the quantity of drinking water in the vessel's tanks
A device that measures air humidity
A device that measures the specific gravity of the battery electrolyte
The hydrostatic release mechanism of the radio beacon

items.explanation: A hydrometer measures the specific gravity of the electrolyte, indicating a lead-acid battery's state of charge.

items.prompt: During a prolonged distress event you hear the call: "All Stations Seelonce Mayday". What does it mean?

options.text: The controlling station is silencing stations interfering with the distress traffic
The controlling station is ending the distress event and releasing Channel 16 for routine working
The coast station or RCC near the event is requesting help from more vessels in the area
A message to all vessels that limited, careful working is allowed on Channel 16

items.explanation: SEELONCE MAYDAY imposes radio silence on all stations except those handling the distress.

items.prompt: What is the order of magnitude of the position accuracy obtained for a COSPAS-SARSAT beacon relying on the Doppler effect?

options.text: A radius of 5 to 50 metres
A radius of about 5 km
A radius of about 50 miles
A beacon defined as Class D allows GPS accuracy

items.explanation: Doppler-only positioning gives an accuracy on the order of ~5 km; GPS-encoded beacons are far more precise.

items.prompt: Which VHF radiotelephone working channel would you propose to a ship station for clearing the way (passing arrangements)?

options.text: CH06
CH70
CH13
CH15

items.explanation: Ship-to-ship passing/navigation-safety arrangements use the bridge-to-bridge channel Ch13.

items.prompt: What determines VHF range?

options.text: The transmission channel
Height of the shore transmitting antenna
Answers b + d are correct
Height of the ship's transmitting antenna

items.explanation: VHF is line-of-sight, so range depends on both antenna heights — the shore antenna (b) and the ship's antenna (d).

items.prompt: A COSPAS-SARSAT type radio beacon will be received by:

options.text: RCC
MCC
Leolut or Geolut
Leosar or Geosar

items.explanation: The beacon signal is first received by the SAR satellites — LEOSAR (low-earth-orbit) or GEOSAR (geostationary) — which relay it to the LUT.

items.prompt: When calling the Napoli coast station, you use the expression:

options.text: Napoli Port
Napoli Station
Napoli Harbor
Napoli Radio

items.explanation: Coast stations are addressed by the place name followed by 'Radio' — e.g. Napoli Radio.

items.prompt: Between two yachts you can establish communication on the following channels:

options.text: CH12, CH16, CH20
CH11, CH70, CH20
CH12, CH24, CH20
CH11, CH15, CH13

items.explanation: Of the options, only d lists channels usable for direct (simplex working / on-board / bridge-to-bridge) communication between vessels; the others include public-correspondence, calling or DSC channels.

items.prompt: To which device / maritime radio station does the identity 970881234 belong?

options.text: AIS
